conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

First, do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I have a child, Alex, who's 8 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. 

To help determine your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask you a few questions.

First, are you currently living in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. 

Next question: Does Alex normally live with you, or are you the person mainly responsible for Alex's care?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't live with me. They live elsewhere, but I do contribute towards their upkeep.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. 

Can you tell me who Alex normally lives with? And is that person claiming Child Benefit for Alex, or do you know if anyone else is currently receiving Child Benefit for them?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex lives with their other parent. As far as I know, no one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information.

Since Alex lives with their other parent, I need to understand a bit more about the situation. Are you contributing towards Alex's upkeep by making regular payments (such as child maintenance), or are you providing for them in some other way?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I make regular payments towards Alex's upkeep - I help with things like clothes, school expenses, and other costs they need.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for explaining that.

One more important question: Do you know why the other parent (who Alex lives with) is not claiming Child Benefit for them? Is there a specific reason they're not making a claim?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'm not entirely sure why they haven't claimed it. They just haven't applied for it as far as I know.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I understand, thank you for all that information.

Just to make sure I have a complete picture: Is there anything else about your situation or Alex's circumstances that you think might be relevant? For example:

- Is Alex in full-time non-advanced education or approved training?
- Are there any special circumstances regarding Alex's living arrangements or care?
- Is there any reason why the parent Alex lives with couldn't or shouldn't claim Child Benefit?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nothing like that. Alex is only 8, so they're just in primary school - normal school for their age. They live with their other parent full-time, and there's no special circumstances or reasons why the other parent couldn't claim it. It's just a straightforward situation where Alex lives with them and I contribute financially towards Alex's needs.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on what you've told me, the key principle of Child Benefit is that generally **only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child**, and priority is normally given to the person the child lives with.

Since Alex lives full-time with their other parent, that parent would have the primary claim to Child Benefit for Alex - even if they haven't actually applied for it yet. The fact that you contribute financially towards Alex's upkeep through regular payments is important and commendable, but it doesn't override the fact that Alex doesn't live with you.

Child Benefit is typically awarded to the person who has the child living with them or who has main responsibility for the child's day-to-day care. Contributing financially alone, when the child lives elsewhere with another parent, wouldn't meet the eligibility criteria.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry, but based on your circumstances, **you would not be eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex**.

The other parent who Alex lives with would be the one entitled to claim Child Benefit, as they have Alex living with them and are responsible for their day-to-day care. If they haven't claimed it, they may wish to apply, as Child Benefit can be backdated for up to 3 months from the date of the claim.

Is there anything else I can help clarify about Child Benefit?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old and lives full-time with their other parent. While the claimant contributes financially towards Alex's upkeep through regular payments, they do not have Alex living with them. Under Child Benefit rules, only one person can claim for a child, and priority is given to the person the child normally lives with. Since Alex lives with their other parent, that parent would have the primary entitlement to claim Child Benefit for Alex, even if they haven't applied. The claimant's financial contributions alone do not qualify them for Child Benefit when the child lives elsewhere with another parent who has day-to-day care responsibility.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant contributes financially to their 8-year-old child Alex but Alex lives full-time with the other parent. Child Benefit priority goes to the person the child lives with, so the claimant is not eligible to claim for Alex.

Since Alex lives with their other parent, that parent would have the primary entitlement to claim Child Benefit for Alex, even if they haven't applied. The claimant's financial contributions alone do not qualify them for Child Benefit when the child lives elsewhere with another parent who has day-to-day care responsibility.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant contributes financially to their 8-year-old child Alex but Alex lives full-time with the other parent. Child Benefit priority goes to the person the child lives with, so the claimant is not eligible to claim for Alex.
